about.built_p1: am-i.exposed is built and maintained by Copexit and Arkad (@multicripto). Privacy is a right, not a feature. This project has no VC funding, no token, and no business model beyond Value for Value.
about.contribute_p1: This is an open-source project and contributions are welcome. Whether it's fixing a bug, improving a heuristic, adding a translation, or just starring the repo - every bit helps.
about.cta: Ready to check your privacy? Scan a transaction or address to see what chain analysis can infer.
about.principle_client_desc: All analysis runs in your browser. No server processes your data. No backend stores your queries.
about.principle_free_desc: No paywalls, no premium tiers, no token. Bitcoin privacy tools should be accessible to everyone.
about.principle_no_tracking_desc: No analytics, no cookies, no fingerprinting. What you scan is never visible to anyone.
about.principle_open_source_desc: Every line of code is auditable. MIT licensed. Fork it, self-host it, improve it.
about.principle_v4v_desc: If it provides value, send value back via Lightning. No pressure, no guilt, no accounts.
about.story_p1: In April 2024, OXT.me and KYCP.org went offline following the arrest of the Samourai Wallet developers. OXT was the gold standard for Boltzmann entropy analysis. KYCP made CoinJoin privacy assessment accessible to ordinary users. Both are gone.
about.story_p2: Chain surveillance firms like Chainalysis, Elliptic, and Crystal still have their tools. They analyze every transaction on the Bitcoin blockchain. They cluster addresses, trace fund flows, and flag wallets.
about.story_p3: But as of that moment, ordinary Bitcoin users had no way to see what these firms could infer about their own transactions. The asymmetry was total: they could see everything about you, and you could see nothing about yourself.
about.story_p4: am-i.exposed was built to close that gap.
about.subtitle: The Bitcoin privacy tools the community relied on are gone. This is the replacement.
about.title: Why This Exists
about.what_p1: Paste any Bitcoin address or transaction ID. The tool runs 30 heuristics against it - the same techniques chain analysis firms use - and shows you exactly what they can infer.
about.what_p2: You get a privacy score from 0 to 100, a letter grade, and specific findings with actionable recommendations. Not just 'your privacy is bad' but 'here is why, and here is what to do about it.'

faq.a_address_reuse: Address reuse creates deterministic, irrefutable links between all transactions using that address. It carries the harshest penalty in privacy scoring. Most modern wallets generate a new address for each receive to avoid this. If you are reusing addresses, switch to a wallet that supports HD key derivation.
faq.a_change_detection:
When you send Bitcoin, the leftover amount (change) is sent back to your own wallet. Chain analysis firms use several techniques to identify which output is the change: (1) Address type mismatch - if inputs are all bc1q but one output is a different type, the matching output is likely change. (2) Round amounts - a round payment (e.g. 0.01 BTC) next to a non-round output reveals which is the payment and which is change. (3) Necessary vs. unnecessary inputs - when all inputs are required to cover the payment amount, the larger output is likely the payment and the smaller output is change (subset sum analysis). Adding unnecessary inputs actually creates ambiguity that makes change detection harder, but it exposes more of your wallet via the Common Input Ownership Heuristic (CIOH), linking additional UTXOs to you. (4) Peel chains - repeatedly spending a large coin and paying with the change creates a traceable chain of decreasing outputs. This is a common pattern: send 2.0 BTC, get 1.8 back as change, spend 1.8, get 1.6 back, and so on. Each step peels off a small payment while the change shrinks predictably. Combined with consolidation (merging coins back together), this is the worst of both worlds - each payee learns about your full transaction chain. (5) nLockTime and nVersion fingerprinting - different wallets sign with different version values, so if the change output was created by a different wallet than the payment output, the mismatch reveals which is change. (6) Output ordering (BIP 69) - some wallets used to place change in a fixed position, making it trivially identifiable. (7) Script type mismatch - paying from native SegWit to a multisig address makes the type difference obvious.

Change detection combined with address reuse are the two most devastating heuristics for privacy - together they allow an observer to trace the full flow of funds.

To protect yourself: use a wallet that sends change to the same address type (Ashigaru does this automatically; Sparrow warns about mismatches but does not correct them), avoid round payment amounts, use coin control to select specific UTXOs, and consider Stonewall or PayJoin transactions which break the change heuristic entirely.
faq.a_check_privacy: Paste your Bitcoin address or transaction ID into am-i.exposed. The tool analyzes it using 30 heuristics - the same techniques chain analysis firms use - and gives you a privacy score from 0 to 100 with a letter grade (A+ to F) and specific actionable findings. Everything runs in your browser with no tracking.
faq.a_cioh:
If a Bitcoin transaction spends multiple inputs, all inputs are assumed to belong to the same entity. This is the foundational clustering heuristic used by chain surveillance firms like Chainalysis and Elliptic to link addresses together. CoinJoin is the primary way to break this assumption.

Note that UTXO consolidation - commonly recommended by influencers focused on fee efficiency - is harmful to privacy. Combining multiple coins into one transaction links all those inputs to the same entity via CIOH. Consolidation combined with peel chains (always paying with change from a single large coin) is the worst of both worlds: each payee can see your consolidation and trace subsequent payments.
faq.a_coinjoin: Yes. CoinJoin is the most effective technique for improving on-chain privacy. It breaks the common-input-ownership heuristic by combining inputs from multiple independent participants. Whirlpool and WabiSabi CoinJoin transactions regularly score A+ on am-i.exposed.
faq.a_data: No. There is no server, no database, and no analytics. The static site is served from GitHub Pages. Your addresses and transactions are never logged, stored, or transmitted to anyone. The only external requests go to mempool.space for blockchain data (or your own instance if configured).
faq.a_dust_attack: A dust attack sends tiny amounts of Bitcoin (dust) to your addresses. If you later spend that dust alongside your other UTXOs, you link those addresses together - giving the attacker a map of your wallet. am-i.exposed detects dust outputs and warns you not to spend them.
faq.a_oxt_kycp: OXT.me and KYCP.org went offline in April 2024 following the arrest of the Samourai Wallet developers. OXT was the gold standard for Boltzmann entropy analysis. KYCP made CoinJoin privacy assessment accessible to ordinary users. am-i.exposed was created to fill the gap left by these tools.
faq.a_safe: All analysis runs client-side in your browser. There is no server, no accounts, no cookies, and no tracking. However, your browser makes API requests to mempool.space to fetch blockchain data, which means their servers can see your IP and queries. For maximum privacy, use Tor Browser or connect your own node.
faq.a_scoring: Every analysis starts from a base score of 70. Each of the 30 heuristics applies a positive or negative modifier based on what it detects. The sum is clamped to 0-100. Only CoinJoin participation, Taproot usage, and high entropy can raise the score. Everything else can only lower it. Grades: A+ (90-100), B (75-89), C (50-74), D (25-49), F (0-24).
faq.a_tor: Yes. When you use Tor Browser, am-i.exposed auto-detects it and routes API requests through the mempool.space .onion endpoint. This hides which addresses you are querying from mempool.space. For even stronger privacy, connect your own mempool instance via the Setup Guide.
faq.a_traceable: Most Bitcoin transactions are partially traceable. Chain analysis firms use heuristics like common-input-ownership, change detection, and address reuse to trace fund flows. am-i.exposed runs 30 of these heuristics client-side to show you exactly what surveillance firms can infer about your transactions.
faq.a_traced_to_me: Bitcoin is pseudonymous, not anonymous. If any address you control has ever been linked to your identity - through an exchange, a merchant, or public posting - chain analysis can follow the trail to your other addresses. The more you reuse addresses and make round-amount payments, the easier it is.

finding.coin-select-toxic-change.recomme: Consider absorbing change into fee, or select different UTXOs.

finding.consolidation-cross-type.descrip: This consolidation combines UTXOs from {{typeCount}} different script types ({{types}}). This links addresses from different wallet generations or software together, giving chain observers additional clustering signals beyond basic CIOH.
finding.consolidation-cross-type.recomme: Keep UTXOs from different address types separate. If you must consolidate across types, CoinJoin each type independently first.
finding.consolidation-cross-type.title: Cross-type consolidation ({{typeCount}} script types)

finding.consolidation-fan-in.recommendat: If consolidation is necessary, run UTXOs through CoinJoin first to break address linkage. Consider splitting consolidation across multiple smaller transactions over time.
finding.consolidation-fan-in.title: Consolidation: {{inputCount}} inputs to 1 output
finding.consolidation-fan-out.descriptio: This transaction sends from 1 input to {{outputCount}} outputs. This pattern is common in exchange or service batch withdrawals, revealing that the sender is making multiple payments simultaneously.
finding.consolidation-fan-out.recommenda: Batch payments are efficient but reveal payment patterns. For privacy, consider making individual transactions or using PayJoin.
finding.consolidation-fan-out.title: Batch payment pattern: 1 input to {{outputCount}} outputs
finding.consolidation-ratio-anomaly.desc: This transaction has {{inputCount}} inputs but only {{outputCount}} outputs (ratio {{ratio}}:1). A normal payment uses 1-2 inputs. The high input count suggests consolidation is being combined with a payment.
finding.consolidation-ratio-anomaly.reco: Separate consolidation from payments. Consolidate UTXOs in a dedicated transaction (ideally after CoinJoin), then make payments from the consolidated output.
finding.consolidation-ratio-anomaly.titl: Anomalous I/O ratio: {{inputCount}} inputs to {{outputCount}} outputs
finding.dust-attack.description: This transaction sends a tiny amount ({{totalDustValue}} sats) - a common tracking technique. Attackers send small amounts to your address and watch when you spend them, revealing which addresses belong to you. Do NOT spend this with your other funds.
finding.dust-attack.recommendation: Freeze this tiny output in your wallet so it's never spent. If you must get rid of it, send it through CoinJoin or to a throwaway wallet first.
finding.dust-attack.title: Possible dust attack ({{totalDustValue}} sats)
finding.dust-attack.title_coinjoin: Possible dust attack ({{totalDustValue}} sats) (CoinJoin - likely coordinator fee)
finding.dust-outputs.description: This transaction has {{dustCount}} tiny outputs below {{threshold}} sats (total: {{totalDustValue}} sats). These are too small to be worth spending and may indicate tracking dust or a wallet that cannot calculate change properly.
finding.dust-outputs.recommendation: Be careful spending tiny outputs. Keep them separate from your main funds to avoid linking your addresses together.
finding.dust-outputs.title_coinjoin: {{dustCount}} dust outputs detected (CoinJoin - likely coordinator fee)
finding.exchange-withdrawal-pattern.desc: This transaction has {{outputCount}} outputs with structural indicators of a centralized exchange batch withdrawal: {{signals}}. Exchange-origin UTXOs carry KYC linkage risk - the exchange knows your identity and can link your withdrawal to your account.
finding.exchange-withdrawal-pattern.reco: If these funds came from a KYC exchange, they are linked to your identity. Before spending, pass them through CoinJoin to break the chain analysis link. Never mix exchange-origin UTXOs with non-KYC UTXOs in the same transaction.
finding.h-coin-selection-bnb.description: Multiple inputs but no change output. Consistent with BnB coin selection. Changeless transactions are better for privacy.
finding.h-coin-selection-bnb.recommendat: Continue using wallets with BnB coin selection.
finding.h-coin-selection-bnb.title: Changeless transaction (possible Branch-and-Bound selection)
finding.h-coin-selection-value-asc.descr: Inputs sorted smallest to largest, which may fingerprint wallet software.
finding.h-coin-selection-value-asc.recom: Consider wallets with randomized input ordering.
finding.h-coin-selection-value-asc.title: Inputs ordered by ascending value
finding.h-coin-selection-value-desc.desc: Inputs sorted largest to smallest, which may fingerprint wallet software.
finding.h-coin-selection-value-desc.reco: Consider wallets with randomized input ordering.
finding.h1-round-amount.description: {{count}} of {{total}} outputs are round numbers (like 0.01 BTC). Round amounts make it obvious which output is the payment and which is your change.
finding.h1-round-amount.description_coin: Equal round outputs are expected in CoinJoin transactions - they are the denomination, not a privacy leak.
finding.h1-round-amount.recommendation: Avoid sending round BTC amounts. Many wallets let you send exact sat amounts. Even adding a few random sats helps obscure the payment amount.
finding.h1-round-amount.title: {{count}} round amount output detected
finding.h1-round-eur-amount.description: {{count}} of {{total}} outputs correspond to round EUR amounts ({{eurValues}}) at the BTC price when this transaction was confirmed (~EUR{{eurPrice}}/BTC). People commonly send round fiat amounts, making these outputs likely payments and the rest change.
finding.h1-round-eur-amount.recommendati: Avoid sending exact euro amounts. When buying BTC, withdraw the full amount rather than a round fiat value. Add a random offset to the payment amount to obscure fiat-denominated rounding.
finding.h1-round-eur-amount.title: {{count}} round EUR amount output detected
finding.h1-round-usd-amount.description: {{count}} of {{total}} outputs correspond to round USD amounts ({{usdValues}}) at the BTC price when this transaction was confirmed (~${{usdPrice}}/BTC). People commonly send round fiat amounts, making these outputs likely payments and the rest change.
finding.h1-round-usd-amount.recommendati: Avoid sending exact dollar amounts. When buying BTC, withdraw the full amount rather than a round fiat value. Add a random offset to the payment amount to obscure fiat-denominated rounding.
finding.h1-round-usd-amount.title: {{count}} round USD amount output detected
finding.h10-p2pkh.description: This is an older address format that reveals more information than necessary. It also has higher transaction fees.
finding.h10-p2pkh.recommendation: Upgrade to a Native SegWit (bc1q) wallet for the best anonymity set and lower fees.
finding.h10-p2pkh.title: Legacy address (P2PKH)
finding.h10-p2sh.description: This address format reveals its type when you spend from it, making your transactions easier to identify.
finding.h10-p2sh.recommendation: Upgrade to a Native SegWit (bc1q) wallet for the best anonymity set and lower fees.
finding.h10-p2sh.title: Pay-to-Script-Hash address (P2SH)
finding.h10-p2tr.description: Taproot hides script complexity - single-sig, multisig, and timelocks all look identical on-chain. This is especially valuable for multisig and complex scripts. For single-sig, P2TR's anonymity set is currently smaller than P2WPKH (native SegWit), which remains the most common address type.
finding.h10-p2tr.recommendation: Taproot is ideal for multisig and complex scripts. For single-sig wallets, P2WPKH (bc1q) currently offers a larger anonymity set. Both are strong choices.
finding.h10-p2tr.title: Taproot address (P2TR)
finding.h10-p2wpkh.description: This is the most widely used modern address format. Because so many people use it, your transactions blend in with the crowd - great for privacy.
finding.h10-p2wpkh.recommendation: P2WPKH has the largest anonymity set for single-sig transactions. No change needed.
finding.h10-p2wpkh.title: Native SegWit address (P2WPKH)
finding.h10-p2wsh.description: This address format is used for multi-key setups. It reveals the spending conditions when you spend, which reduces privacy compared to Taproot.
finding.h10-p2wsh.recommendation: Consider upgrading to a Taproot-based multisig setup (MuSig2 or FROST) for improved privacy.
finding.h10-p2wsh.title: Native SegWit multisig address (P2WSH)
finding.h11-legacy-version.description: This transaction uses nVersion=1, which is uncommon in modern wallets. This narrows identification to legacy software or Wasabi Wallet.
finding.h11-legacy-version.recommendatio: Use a wallet that creates nVersion=2 transactions (BIP68-compliant). Most modern wallets (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, Ashigaru) use nVersion=2.
finding.h11-legacy-version.title: Legacy transaction version (nVersion=1)
finding.h11-mixed-sequence.description: This transaction uses different nSequence values across its inputs. Consistent nSequence values are expected from a single wallet. Mixed values may indicate coin control with manual overrides or multi-party construction.
finding.h11-mixed-sequence.recommendatio: Standard wallets use uniform nSequence across all inputs. If using coin control, ensure sequence values are consistent.
finding.h11-mixed-sequence.title: Mixed nSequence values across inputs
finding.h11-no-locktime.description: This transaction has nLockTime=0, meaning it lacks anti-fee-sniping protection. Most modern wallets set nLockTime to the current block height. Not using it fingerprints the wallet and slightly weakens network security.
finding.h11-no-locktime.recommendation: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum, and Ashigaru all do this).
finding.h11-no-locktime.title: No anti-fee-sniping protection (nLockTime=0)
finding.h11-wallet-fingerprint.descripti: This transaction contains clues about which wallet software was used: {{signals}}. Knowing the wallet helps analysts narrow down who you might be.
This transaction contains clues about which wallet software was used: {{signals}}. These patterns match {{walletGuess}}. Knowing the wallet helps analysts narrow down who you might be.
finding.h11-wallet-fingerprint.recommend: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - the goal is not invisibility but blending in. Wallets with millions of users (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, Electrum) create large anonymity sets where your transaction looks like millions of others. Niche wallets create small sets that narrow identification. The fingerprint itself is unavoidable; what matters is how many people share it.
finding.h11-wallet-fingerprint.title_sig: {{signalCount}} wallet fingerprinting signal detected (CoinJoin - less relevant)
{{signalCount}} wallet fingerprinting signals detected (CoinJoin - less relevant)
finding.h13-ofac-match.description: This address matches an entry on the U.S. Treasury OFAC Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) list. Transacting with sanctioned addresses may have serious legal consequences.
finding.h13-ofac-match.recommendation: Do NOT send funds to this address. Consult legal counsel if you have already transacted with this address.
finding.h13-ofac-match.title: OFAC sanctioned address
finding.h13-presend-check.description: Destination address was checked for reuse, sanctions, and other privacy risks.
finding.h13-presend-check.recommendation: Review the risk assessment above and decide whether to proceed with this transaction.
This destination looks clean. You can proceed with your transaction.
Ask the recipient for a fresh, unused address. If this is an exchange, consider the privacy implications.
finding.h13-presend-check.title: Destination risk: {{riskLevel}}
finding.h17-escrow-2of2.description: This transaction spends from a 2-of-2 multisig input to exactly 2 outputs, a pattern consistent with P2P exchange escrow releases or Lightning Network cooperative channel closes. The 2-of-2 structure reveals that two parties had to sign.
finding.h17-escrow-2of2.recommendation: 2-of-2 multisig escrow reveals multi-party involvement on-chain. For future P2P trades, consider protocols using Taproot MuSig that hides the multisig structure. For Lightning, cooperative closes are normal and expected.
finding.h17-escrow-2of2.title: 2-of-2 multisig escrow detected
finding.h17-escrow-2of3.description: This transaction spends from a 2-of-3 multisig input, consistent with P2P exchange escrow (HodlHodl), cold storage (Unchained, Casa, Nunchuk), or business escrow. The script reveals three parties share custody.
finding.h17-escrow-2of3.recommendation: If using multisig for cold storage, consider migrating to Taproot-based multisig (MuSig2 or FROST) which looks identical to single-sig on-chain.
finding.h17-escrow-2of3.title: 2-of-3 multisig escrow detected
finding.h17-hodlhodl.description: This transaction matches the HodlHodl P2P exchange pattern: a 2-of-3 multisig input with a small fee output to a known HodlHodl fee address. This identifies the transaction as a P2P exchange escrow release with high confidence.
finding.h17-hodlhodl.recommendation: HodlHodl escrow transactions are identifiable on-chain. For more private P2P trading, consider Lightning-based settlement (e.g., RoboSats).
finding.h17-hodlhodl.title: Likely HodlHodl escrow release (2-of-3 multisig)
finding.h17-multisig-info.description: This transaction spends from {{inputCount}} wrapped multisig input(s) ({{types}}). The M-of-N configuration is revealed when the multisig is spent, exposing the multi-party nature of the input.
finding.h17-multisig-info.recommendation: Use Taproot (P2TR) with MuSig2 or FROST for multisig that is indistinguishable from single-sig on-chain.
finding.h17-multisig-info.title: Wrapped multisig detected: {{types}}
finding.h2-change-detected.description: {{signalCount}} patterns suggest which output is your change. When your change is identified, the exact payment amount and recipient are revealed.
finding.h2-change-detected.recommendatio: Avoid sending round amounts. Use the same address type for all outputs. Consistent address formats make change harder to spot.
finding.h2-change-detected.title: Change output likely identifiable ({{confidence}} confidence)
finding.h2-change-detected.title_coinjoi: Change output likely identifiable ({{confidence}} confidence) (CoinJoin - unreliable)
finding.h2-same-address-io.description: An address that funded this transaction also receives an output. This is a 100% deterministic link - the output to this address is certainly change, revealing which other outputs are payments and the exact payment amount.
finding.h2-same-address-io.recommendatio: Use a wallet that generates a new change address for every transaction (HD wallets). Never send change back to the same address. Sparrow, Wasabi, and Bitcoin Core all handle this correctly.
finding.h2-same-address-io.title: Same address in input and output - change revealed ({{matchCount}} of {{totalSpendable}} outputs)
finding.h2-self-send.description: {{matchCount}} of {{totalSpendable}} spendable outputs go back to an address that was also an input. This is a severe privacy failure - it reveals which output is the change and therefore the exact payment amount.
finding.h2-self-send.description_all: Every spendable output goes back to an address that was also an input. This reveals the sender's exact balance and creates a trivial on-chain link.
finding.h2-self-send.recommendation: Use a wallet that generates a new change address for every transaction (HD wallets). Never send change back to the same address.
finding.h2-self-send.title: {{matchCount}} of {{totalSpendable}} outputs sent back to input address
finding.h2-self-send.title_all: All outputs return to input address
finding.h2-sweep.description: This transaction has exactly 1 input and 1 output (plus fee). There is no change output, meaning the entire input is sent to the output. Entropy is 0 bits - the link between sender and receiver is completely deterministic.
finding.h2-sweep.recommendation: Avoid full-sweep transactions when privacy is needed. Consider splitting funds across multiple transactions or using CoinJoin before sweeping.
finding.h2-sweep.title: Sweep transaction - 100% deterministic link
finding.h2-wallet-hop.description: Input script type(s) ({{fromTypes}}) differ from the output type ({{toType}}), suggesting a wallet migration or address type upgrade.
finding.h2-wallet-hop.recommendation: Wallet migrations link all inputs together. Consider using CoinJoin before consolidating to break linkability.
finding.h2-wallet-hop.title: Address type upgrade detected (possible wallet migration)
finding.h3-cioh.description: This transaction combines funds from {{count}} different addresses. Analysts will assume all {{count}} addresses belong to you. This is one of the most powerful tracking techniques.
finding.h3-cioh.description_coinjoin: Multiple input addresses are linked, but this is expected in a CoinJoin transaction. In CoinJoins, each input typically belongs to a different participant, so CIOH does not apply.
finding.h3-cioh.recommendation: Avoid combining funds from different addresses in one transaction. If you must, use CoinJoin first to break the link.
finding.h3-cioh.title: {{count}} input addresses linked by CIOH
finding.h3-cioh.title_coinjoin: {{count}} input addresses linked by CIOH (CoinJoin - expected)
finding.h3-single-input.description: This transaction only uses one address as input, so there's no way to link multiple addresses together from this transaction alone.
finding.h3-single-input.recommendation: Keep using single-input transactions when possible.
finding.h3-single-input.title: Single input address
finding.h4-coinjoin.description: {{count}} of {{total}} outputs have the same value ({{denomination}}). This is a CoinJoin - a collaborative transaction where multiple people mix their funds together, making it very hard to trace.
finding.h4-coinjoin.description_wabisabi: This transaction has {{vin}} inputs and {{vout}} outputs with {{groups}} groups of equal values - a WabiSabi CoinJoin (Wasabi Wallet). Multiple people mixed their funds together at different amounts, making tracing very difficult.
finding.h4-coinjoin.recommendation: CoinJoin is one of the best privacy tools available. For maximum benefit, do multiple rounds of mixing.
finding.h4-coinjoin.recommendation_wabis: WabiSabi CoinJoins provide excellent privacy through large anonymity sets and multiple denomination tiers. Continue using CoinJoin for maximum privacy.
finding.h4-coinjoin.title: Likely CoinJoin: {{count}} equal outputs of {{denomination}}
finding.h4-coinjoin.title_wabisabi: WabiSabi CoinJoin: {{groups}} denomination tiers, {{totalEqual}} equal outputs across {{vout}} total
finding.h4-exchange-flagging.description: Some exchanges flag or freeze accounts linked to CoinJoin transactions - sometimes months or years later. In documented cases, exchanges have flagged CoinJoins retroactively and even closed accounts for coins that had CoinJoin history from a previous owner.
finding.h4-exchange-flagging.recommendat: Don't send CoinJoin outputs directly to an exchange. Use decentralized exchanges (Bisq, RoboSats, Hodl Hodl) that don't monitor your transaction history. Keep privacy wallets and exchange wallets separate.
finding.h4-exchange-flagging.title: Exchanges may flag this transaction
finding.h4-joinmarket.description: This transaction has {{vin}} inputs from {{count}} addresses and {{count}} equal outputs of {{denomination}} - consistent with a JoinMarket CoinJoin. Multiple people pooled their funds, making it impossible to tell who paid whom.
finding.h4-joinmarket.recommendation: JoinMarket provides good privacy through its decentralized maker/taker model. For stronger privacy, consider multiple rounds of mixing.
finding.h4-joinmarket.title: Likely JoinMarket CoinJoin: {{count}} equal outputs of {{denomination}}
finding.h4-payjoin.description: This transaction has inputs from {{distinctAddresses}} distinct addresses and 2 outputs, consistent with a PayJoin (Stowaway) pattern. A chain analyst would assume a payment of {{probableAmount}} sats, but the real amount is likely different because the receiver contributed {{receiverContribution}} sats. PayJoin breaks all standard change detection heuristics.
finding.h4-payjoin.recommendation: PayJoin is one of the strongest privacy techniques available. It makes the transaction indistinguishable from a regular payment while hiding the real amount. Continue using PayJoin-compatible wallets.
finding.h4-payjoin.title: Possible PayJoin / Stowaway detected
finding.h4-simplified-stonewall.descript: This transaction has 3 outputs: 2 equal-value outputs ({{denomination}}) and 1 change output. This is a simplified Stonewall - a manual decoy technique where the sender creates a second output of the same amount to create ambiguity about which is the real payment.
finding.h4-simplified-stonewall.recommen: Simplified Stonewall provides basic ambiguity but is weaker than a full Stonewall. When possible, use Ashigaru's built-in Stonewall for stronger privacy guarantees.
finding.h4-simplified-stonewall.title: Simplified Stonewall: 2 equal outputs of {{denomination}} + change
finding.h4-stonewall.description: This transaction matches the solo Stonewall pattern: inputs from 1 wallet, 4 outputs with 2 equal values ({{denomination}}). Stonewall creates genuine ambiguity: an observer cannot tell if this is a single-wallet payment or a two-wallet collaborative transaction.
finding.h4-stonewall.recommendation: Stonewall provides real privacy by creating ambiguity. The critical post-transaction rule: never spend two outputs from this transaction together.
finding.h4-stonewall.title: Stonewall detected: 2 equal outputs of {{denomination}}
finding.h4-stonewallx2.description: This transaction matches the STONEWALLx2 pattern: inputs from {{distinctAddresses}} distinct addresses, 4 outputs with 2 equal values ({{denomination}}). STONEWALLx2 is a real 2-person collaborative transaction where each party contributes inputs, providing stronger privacy than solo Stonewall.
finding.h4-stonewallx2.recommendation: STONEWALLx2 provides strong privacy through real multi-party collaboration. Never spend two outputs from this transaction together. The mining fee is split 50/50 between participants.
finding.h4-stonewallx2.title: STONEWALLx2 detected: 2-person CoinJoin ({{denomination}})
finding.h4-whirlpool.description: This matches a Whirlpool CoinJoin pattern: 5 equal outputs at a standard amount. Whirlpool breaks the connection between your inputs and outputs, giving strong privacy.
finding.h4-whirlpool.recommendation: Whirlpool is one of the strongest CoinJoin implementations. Make sure to also remix (multiple rounds) for maximum privacy.
finding.h4-whirlpool.title: Whirlpool CoinJoin detected ({{denom}} pool)
finding.h5-entropy.description: This transaction has {{entropy}} bits of entropy (via {{method}}), meaning there are ~{{interpretations}} possible ways the funds could have flowed. More possibilities = harder to trace. Entropy per UTXO: {{entropyPerUtxo}} bits ({{nUtxos}} UTXOs).
finding.h5-entropy.recommendation_coinjo: Strong entropy from this CoinJoin. Avoid merging post-mix UTXOs - each merge reduces your anonymity set. If consolidation is necessary, remix first.
finding.h5-entropy.recommendation_high: Good entropy level. Spending exact amounts (no change) further improves privacy.
finding.h5-entropy.recommendation_low: Consider using CoinJoin to make your transactions harder to trace.
finding.h5-entropy.title: Transaction entropy: {{entropy}} bits
finding.h5-low-entropy.description: This transaction has very low entropy ({{entropy}} bits, via {{method}}). There's essentially only one way to interpret the fund flow, making it easy to trace.
finding.h5-low-entropy.recommendation: Transactions that are harder to interpret are harder to trace. CoinJoin maximizes ambiguity.
finding.h5-low-entropy.title: Very low transaction entropy
finding.h5-zero-entropy.description: This transaction has one input and one output - there's only one possible interpretation. The fund flow is completely transparent.
finding.h5-zero-entropy.recommendation: Transactions with more inputs and outputs are naturally harder to trace. CoinJoin maximizes this.
finding.h5-zero-entropy.title: Zero transaction entropy
finding.h6-fee-in-amount.description: An output of {{outputAmount}} sats plus fee ({{feeAmount}} sats) equals {{roundTotal}} sats - a round BTC amount.
finding.h6-fee-in-amount.recommendation: Minor wallet fingerprint. Consider a wallet that calculates fees separately from the send amount.
finding.h6-fee-in-amount.title: Fee appears subtracted from output amount
finding.h6-fee-segwit-miscalc.descriptio: This SegWit transaction's fee aligns to {{rawFeeRate}} sat/byte but not {{segwitFeeRate}} sat/vB, suggesting older wallet software.
finding.h6-fee-segwit-miscalc.recommenda: Use a wallet that properly estimates fees using virtual bytes (vB).
finding.h6-fee-segwit-miscalc.title: Fee appears calculated using non-SegWit weight
finding.h6-rbf-signaled.description: This transaction signals RBF replaceability. RBF is now standard across virtually all modern wallets and is no longer a meaningful fingerprinting signal.
finding.h6-rbf-signaled.recommendation: RBF is standard practice. Nearly all wallets signal RBF by default, so this reveals very little about the sender.
finding.h6-rbf-signaled.title: RBF (Replace-by-Fee) signaled
finding.h6-round-fee-rate.description: This transaction uses a round fee rate of {{feeRate}} sat/vB. Some wallets use round numbers, which can help identify the wallet software.
finding.h6-round-fee-rate.recommendation: This is a minor signal. Most modern wallets now use precise fee estimation.
finding.h6-round-fee-rate.title: Exact fee rate: {{feeRate}} sat/vB
finding.h6-round-fee-rate.title_coinjoin: Exact fee rate: {{feeRate}} sat/vB (CoinJoin - coordinator fee)
finding.h7-op-return.description: This transaction contains embedded data (OP_RETURN) that is permanently visible on the blockchain.
finding.h7-op-return.description_decoded: This transaction contains embedded data (OP_RETURN) that is permanently visible on the blockchain. Decoded text: "{{decoded}}".
finding.h7-op-return.description_protoco: This transaction contains embedded data (OP_RETURN) that is permanently visible on the blockchain. Protocol: {{protocol}}.
finding.h7-op-return.recommendation: OP_RETURN data is permanent and public. Avoid services that embed unnecessary data in your transactions.
finding.h7-op-return.title: OP_RETURN data embedded in transaction
finding.h8-address-reuse.description: This address has been used in {{txCount}} transactions. Every transaction involving this address can be trivially linked together. Address reuse is the #1 privacy mistake in Bitcoin.
finding.h8-address-reuse.recommendation: Use a wallet that generates a new address for every payment you receive. Never share the same address twice.
finding.h8-address-reuse.title: Address reused across {{txCount}} transactions
finding.h8-batch-receive.description: This address received {{totalFunded}} payments in a single transaction (batch payment). This is not address reuse since it's one transaction.
finding.h8-batch-receive.recommendation: This is typically caused by exchange batched withdrawals. Use a fresh address for the next receive.
finding.h8-batch-receive.title: Multiple UTXOs from a single transaction (batch payment)
finding.h8-no-reuse.description: This address has only received funds once. Single-use addresses are a core Bitcoin privacy practice.
finding.h8-no-reuse.recommendation: Keep using fresh addresses for every receive.
finding.h8-no-reuse.title: No address reuse detected
finding.h8-reuse-uncertain.description: This address has {{txCount}} transactions but the receive count ({{totalFunded}}) appears incomplete. The API backend may not fully index funded outputs. Address reuse cannot be confirmed or ruled out.
finding.h8-reuse-uncertain.recommendatio: Try analyzing this address using the public mempool.space API for more complete data.
finding.h8-reuse-uncertain.title: Address reuse data incomplete
finding.h9-clean.description: No suspicious tiny outputs and a manageable number of unspent funds. This is a healthy state.
finding.h9-clean.title: Clean wallet state
finding.h9-dust-detected.description: Found {{dustCount}} tiny outputs below {{threshold}} sats (total: {{totalDust}} sats). These may be tracking dust - tiny amounts sent by surveillance companies. If you spend them alongside your other funds, it links all your addresses together.
finding.h9-dust-detected.recommendation: Do NOT spend these tiny outputs. Freeze them in your wallet's coin control. If your wallet doesn't support this, consider Sparrow or Bitcoin Core.
finding.h9-many-utxos.description: This address has {{utxoCount}} unspent outputs. Spending many of them together in one transaction links them, reducing privacy and increasing fees.
finding.h9-many-utxos.recommendation: Consider consolidating during low-fee periods, or use CoinJoin to break the link before combining them.
finding.h9-many-utxos.title: Many unspent outputs ({{utxoCount}})
finding.h9-moderate-utxos.description: This address has {{utxoCount}} unspent outputs. Be careful when spending - combining them in one transaction reveals they belong to you.
finding.h9-moderate-utxos.recommendation: Select specific outputs when sending instead of letting your wallet auto-select all of them.
finding.h9-moderate-utxos.title: {{utxoCount}} unspent outputs on this address
finding.high-activity-exchange.descripti: This address has {{txCount}} transactions and has received funds {{timesReceived}} times. This volume is consistent with a centralized exchange or payment processor.
finding.high-activity-exchange.recommend: Never reuse deposit addresses. If this is a service, request a fresh address for each transaction.
finding.high-activity-exchange.title: Extremely high activity address ({{txCount}} transactions)
finding.high-activity-moderate.descripti: This address has {{txCount}} transactions. This level of reuse reduces privacy for all parties who have transacted with this address.
finding.high-activity-moderate.recommend: For better privacy, use a new address for each transaction. HD wallets generate fresh addresses automatically.
finding.high-activity-moderate.title: Moderate activity address ({{txCount}} transactions)
finding.high-activity-service.descriptio: This address has {{txCount}} transactions and has received funds {{timesReceived}} times. Multiple senders to this address can be linked to the same entity.
finding.high-activity-service.recommenda: Request a fresh address for each payment to avoid linking your transaction to other senders.
finding.high-activity-service.title: High activity address ({{txCount}} transactions)
No input subset could be matched to a change output. This JoinMarket transaction resists the most common analytical attack.
This CoinJoin provides strong privacy. Continue using JoinMarket for mixing.
Subset-sum attack failed - strong CoinJoin privacy
finding.joinmarket-subset-sum.descriptio: An input subset sums to the taker's expected total (change + denomination + fee). Research shows this attack succeeds ~76% of the time on JoinMarket transactions.
finding.joinmarket-subset-sum.recommenda: JoinMarket's taker privacy is vulnerable to subset-sum attacks. Consider Whirlpool which is immune (no change outputs).
finding.joinmarket-subset-sum.title: Subset-sum attack: taker inputs likely identified ({{takerInputs}} of {{totalInputs}})
finding.joinmarket-taker-maker.descripti: In JoinMarket, the taker pays the full mining fee while makers earn fee income. The smallest change output is the most likely taker change.
finding.joinmarket-taker-maker.recommend: If you are the taker, your change output is more identifiable. Consider mixing it again or converting to Monero/Lightning.
finding.joinmarket-taker-maker.title: Taker/maker analysis: smallest change likely belongs to taker
finding.linkability-ambiguous.descriptio: The linkability matrix shows high ambiguity across input-output pairs.
finding.linkability-ambiguous.recommenda: Good transaction privacy. For even stronger ambiguity, use CoinJoin.
finding.linkability-ambiguous.title: High ambiguity: {{ambiguity}}% average uncertainty
finding.linkability-deterministic.descri: Linkability analysis found {{deterministicLinks}} input-output pair(s) with near-certain connections.
finding.linkability-deterministic.recomm: Use CoinJoin to break deterministic links. Equal outputs create maximum ambiguity.
finding.linkability-deterministic.title: {{deterministicLinks}} deterministic input-output link(s) found
finding.partial-history-partial.descript: This address has {{totalOnChain}} transactions but only the most recent {{txsAnalyzed}} were analyzed. Older transactions may have additional privacy issues.
finding.partial-history-partial.recommen: For a complete analysis, use a local node or API with higher limits.
finding.partial-history-partial.title: Partial history analyzed ({{txsAnalyzed}} of {{totalOnChain}} transactions)
finding.partial-history-unavailable.desc: This address has {{totalOnChain}} transactions but transaction history could not be fetched. Spending pattern analysis could not be performed, so the score may be incomplete.
finding.partial-history-unavailable.reco: Try again later, or use a custom API endpoint with higher rate limits.
finding.peel-chain-trace-short.descripti: A short peel chain of {{hops}} hops was detected. Sequential spending with asymmetric outputs is identifiable.
finding.peel-chain-trace-short.recommend: Vary spending patterns and avoid creating obvious change outputs.
finding.peel-chain-trace-short.title: Short peel chain: {{hops}} hops detected
finding.peel-chain-trace.description: A peel chain of {{hops}} hops was traced. Total of {{totalPeeled}} sats peeled off, {{remainingBalance}} sats remaining.
finding.peel-chain-trace.recommendation: Break peel chains by using CoinJoin before spending change. Use BnB coin selection for changeless transactions.
finding.peel-chain-trace.title: Peel chain: {{hops}} hops traced, {{totalPeeled}} sats peeled
finding.peel-chain.description: This transaction is part of a peel chain - a sequence of {{chainDepth}}+ transactions each with 1 input and 2 outputs, where one output feeds the next transaction as its sole input. The smaller output at each hop is typically the payment, making the entire payment history trivially traceable.
finding.peel-chain.recommendation: Break the linear chain by using CoinJoin between payments. Batch multiple payments into a single transaction, or use PayJoin to add inputs from the recipient and destroy the peel pattern.
finding.peel-chain.title: Peel chain detected ({{chainDepth}}+ consecutive hops)
finding.prospective-fingerprint-change.d: A wallet fingerprint change was detected between transactions. This may indicate a wallet update or migration.
finding.prospective-fingerprint-change.r: When changing wallet software, be aware that the change is visible to chain analysts.
finding.prospective-mixed-fingerprints.d: Transaction fingerprints are inconsistent across time. Inconsistent fingerprints make entity linking easier.
finding.prospective-mixed-fingerprints.r: Use a single wallet with consistent settings for all transactions from the same address cluster.
finding.prospective-script-diversity.des: This address's transactions use {{scriptTypeCount}} different script types. Using many types reveals an upgrade path.
finding.prospective-script-diversity.rec: Standardize on a single script type (preferably P2TR/Taproot) for all spending.
finding.prospective-script-diversity.tit: {{scriptTypeCount}} different script types used over time
finding.prospective-wallet-migration.des: Multiple wallet fingerprint signals changed between transactions, strongly suggesting a wallet migration. Chain analysts can use these transitions to identify software changes.
finding.prospective-wallet-migration.rec: When switching wallets, send funds through a CoinJoin first to prevent fingerprint linking.
finding.recurring-payment-pattern.descri: This address has transacted with {{recurringCount}} counterpart(s) more than once (most frequent: {{maxFrequency}} times). Recurring payments to or from the same address reveal an ongoing financial relationship. A chain analyst can identify regular payment patterns (salary, subscriptions, rent) and use them to profile the address owner.
finding.recurring-payment-pattern.recomm: For recurring payments, use different addresses each time. BIP47 (PayNym) provides reusable payment codes that generate unique addresses per payment. For receiving, provide a fresh address for each invoice. Never reuse addresses for repeated transactions with the same counterparty.
finding.recurring-payment-pattern.title: Recurring payment pattern: {{recurringCount}} repeated counterparty(ies)
finding.script-mixed.description: This transaction uses {{typeCount}} different address formats ({{types}}). Mixing formats makes it easier to identify your change output.
finding.script-mixed.description_coinjoi: Mixed script types are expected in CoinJoin transactions since participants use different wallet software.
finding.script-mixed.recommendation: Use a wallet that keeps the same address format for all outputs. Consistent formats eliminate this signal.
finding.script-mixed.title: {{typeCount}} different script types in transaction
finding.script-mixed.title_coinjoin: {{typeCount}} different script types in transaction (CoinJoin - expected)
finding.script-multisig.description: This transaction has {{count}} exposed multi-signature outputs. This reveals all public keys on the blockchain, making it easy to identify the signers.
finding.script-multisig.recommendation: Use Taproot for multi-key setups - it looks identical to single-key transactions on-chain.
finding.script-uniform.description: All inputs and outputs use the same address format. This makes it harder to identify which output is your change.
finding.script-uniform.recommendation: Continue using wallets that maintain consistent address types.
finding.script-uniform.title: Uniform script types
finding.spending-high-volume.description: This address has {{totalTxs}} transactions. High-activity addresses are more likely to be tracked and may be flagged as belonging to a business or service.
finding.spending-high-volume.recommendat: Spread your activity across multiple addresses. Modern wallets do this automatically.
finding.spending-high-volume.title: High transaction volume ({{totalTxs}} transactions)
finding.spending-many-counterparties.des: This address has transacted with {{counterpartyCount}}+ different addresses. The more connections, the easier it is to identify and track.
finding.spending-many-counterparties.rec: Use different addresses for different people. Modern wallets handle this automatically.
finding.spending-never-spent.description: This address has received funds but never spent them - likely cold storage. No spending patterns can be analyzed.
finding.spending-never-spent.recommendat: When you do spend from this address, use coin control and consider CoinJoin.
finding.spending-never-spent.title: Address has never spent (cold storage)
finding.temporal-burst-high.description: {{burstSize}} transactions were confirmed within a 2-hour window. This burst pattern is characteristic of automated software or batch processing.
finding.temporal-burst-high.recommendati: Space transactions across different blocks and times. Avoid sending multiple transactions in quick succession.
finding.temporal-burst-high.title: {{burstSize}} transactions within 2 hours
finding.temporal-burst-moderate.descript: {{burstSize}} transactions were confirmed within a 2-hour window. This temporal clustering can help analysts correlate activity.
finding.temporal-burst-moderate.recommen: Consider spacing transactions over longer periods to reduce temporal correlation.
finding.temporal-burst-moderate.title: {{burstSize}} transactions within 2 hours
finding.temporal-regular-pattern.descrip: Transactions follow a regular pattern with an average interval of {{intervalHours}} hours. Regular patterns help analysts predict future activity.
finding.temporal-regular-pattern.recomme: Randomize transaction timing. Add random delays to break predictable patterns.
finding.temporal-regular-pattern.title: Regular transaction pattern detected ({{intervalHours}}-hour interval)
finding.timing-locktime-timestamp.descri: This transaction uses an unusual timestamp setting ({{locktime}}). Most wallets don't do this, which makes the transaction stand out.
finding.timing-locktime-timestamp.recomm: Use a wallet that follows standard practices (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow).
finding.timing-locktime-timestamp.title: nLockTime set to timestamp ({{date}})
finding.timing-stale-locktime.descriptio: The nLockTime ({{locktime}}) is {{diff}} blocks before the confirmation height ({{blockHeight}}). This suggests the transaction was created well before it was sent or was intentionally delayed. This can reveal information about how the sender prepares transactions.
finding.timing-stale-locktime.recommenda: Send transactions promptly after creating them.
finding.timing-stale-locktime.title: Transaction held for ~{{diff}} blocks before confirmation
finding.timing-unconfirmed.description: This transaction hasn't been confirmed yet. While waiting in the mempool, anyone watching the network could link your IP address to this transaction.
finding.timing-unconfirmed.recommendatio: Use Tor when sending sensitive transactions. Sparrow and Wasabi support Tor by default.
finding.timing-unconfirmed.title: Transaction is unconfirmed (mempool visible)
finding.tx0-premix.description: This transaction is a Whirlpool tx0 (premix): it splits funds into {{denomCount}} equal outputs of {{denomination}} BTC ready for CoinJoin mixing. {{#hasToxicChange}}The toxic change output ({{toxicChangeValue}} sats) is NOT mixed and must be handled carefully. {{/hasToxicChange}}Coordinator fee: {{coordinatorFee}} sats.
finding.tx0-premix.recommendation: This is the first step of a CoinJoin mix - positive for privacy. If toxic change is present, it must NEVER be spent alongside post-mix (mixed) outputs. Freeze it in your wallet or spend it through a separate mixing cycle.
finding.tx0-premix.title: CoinJoin premix (tx0): {{denomCount}} outputs at {{denomination}} BTC
finding.unnecessary-input.description: This transaction used {{totalInputs}} inputs, but only {{minInputsNeeded}} were needed to cover the outputs and fee. The {{excessInputs}} extra inputs unnecessarily link additional addresses together via CIOH, suggesting poor coin selection or intentional consolidation.
finding.unnecessary-input.recommendation: Use a wallet with coin control to select only the minimum UTXOs needed. Avoid auto-selection modes that sweep all available UTXOs into every transaction.
finding.unnecessary-input.title: {{excessInputs}} unnecessary input(s) used
finding.wallet-address-reuse.description: {{reusedCount}} addresses received funds more than once. Address reuse directly links transactions and reveals spending patterns. {{ratio}}% of active addresses are reused.
finding.wallet-address-reuse.recommendat: Never reuse Bitcoin addresses. Generate a new address for each incoming payment.
finding.wallet-address-reuse.title: {{reusedCount}} of {{totalReceived}} addresses reused
finding.wallet-consolidation-history.des: Consolidation transactions reveal which addresses belong to the same wallet.
finding.wallet-consolidation-history.rec: Minimize consolidations. When necessary, use CoinJoin.
finding.wallet-dust-utxos.description: The wallet contains {{dustCount}} UTXOs below the dust threshold. These are uneconomical to spend and may be dust attack indicators.
finding.wallet-dust-utxos.recommendation: Consolidate dust during low-fee periods, or ignore unsolicited small amounts.
finding.wallet-dust-utxos.title: {{dustCount}} dust UTXOs ({{dustValue}} sats)
finding.wallet-mixed-script-utxos.descri: Active UTXOs exist across multiple script types. Mixing types reveals wallet migration history.
finding.wallet-mixed-script-utxos.recomm: Gradually migrate all UTXOs to a single script type (P2WPKH or P2TR).
finding.wallet-mixed-script-utxos.title: UTXOs across {{scriptTypes}} script types
finding.wallet-no-reuse.description: All {{activeCount}} active addresses were used exactly once. Correct privacy practice.
finding.wallet-no-reuse.title: No address reuse detected
finding.wallet-toxic-change.description: UTXOs between dust threshold and 10,000 sats are uneconomical to spend and can link transactions.
finding.wallet-toxic-change.recommendati: Use exact-match coin selection (BnB) to avoid creating change.
finding.wallet-toxic-change.title: {{toxicCount}} toxic change UTXOs detected
finding.wallet-uniform-script.descriptio: All wallet UTXOs use the same script type, avoiding wallet migration fingerprinting.
finding.wallet-uniform-script.recommenda: Continue using a consistent script type.
finding.wallet-uniform-script.title: Uniform script type: {{scriptType}}
finding.wallet-utxo-bloat.description: A large UTXO set increases fees and exposes more privacy information when spending.
finding.wallet-utxo-bloat.recommendation: Consolidate smaller UTXOs during low-fee periods using coin control.
finding.wallet-utxo-bloat.title: {{totalUtxos}} UTXOs - consider consolidation
finding.witness-deep-stack.description: At least one input has a witness stack with {{maxDepth}} items. Standard single-sig P2WPKH uses 2 items. Deeper stacks indicate complex scripts that fingerprint spending conditions.
finding.witness-deep-stack.recommendatio: Complex witness structures are more identifiable on-chain. When possible, use standard P2WPKH for routine transactions.
finding.witness-deep-stack.title: Unusual witness stack depth ({{maxDepth}} items)
finding.witness-mixed-depths.description: Witness stacks have varying depths ({{depths}} items). This indicates inputs are being spent under different spending conditions.
finding.witness-mixed-depths.recommendat: For better privacy, spend UTXOs with the same script type and complexity together.
finding.witness-mixed-depths.title: Mixed witness stack depths across inputs
finding.witness-mixed-sig-types.descript: This transaction uses {{taprootCount}} Taproot input(s) with Schnorr signatures and {{ecdsaCount}} SegWit v0 input(s) with ECDSA signatures. Mixing signature types strongly fingerprints the wallet.
finding.witness-mixed-sig-types.recommen: Complete the migration to Taproot (P2TR) for all UTXOs before spending them together.
finding.witness-mixed-sig-types.title: Mixed Schnorr and ECDSA signatures
finding.witness-mixed-types.description: This transaction has {{witnessCount}} SegWit input(s) with witness data and {{nonWitnessCount}} legacy input(s) without. Mixing input types reveals an upgrade path or multi-wallet construction.
finding.witness-mixed-types.recommendati: Use a single address type for all inputs. Migrate legacy UTXOs to SegWit before spending them alongside native SegWit inputs.
finding.witness-mixed-types.title: Mixed witness and non-witness inputs
finding.witness-uniform-size.description: All witness stacks have identical total sizes, suggesting intentional padding for privacy.
finding.witness-uniform-size.recommendat: This is a positive privacy practice. Continue using this wallet's witness handling.
finding.witness-uniform-size.title: Uniform witness data sizes detected
glossary.cta: Ready to analyze a transaction? See these concepts in action.
glossary.def_address_reuse: Using the same Bitcoin address for multiple transactions. Creates deterministic links between all transactions involving that address, severely degrading privacy.
glossary.def_bip47: A protocol that allows two parties to create a shared secret from which unique addresses are derived for each payment. Prevents address reuse without requiring out-of-band address exchange.
glossary.def_bisq: A decentralized, non-custodial Bitcoin exchange operating as a peer-to-peer network with no central server. Bisq trades use 2-of-2 multisig escrow transactions on-chain and send trade fees to publicly known DAO addresses, making them identifiable by chain analysis. Unlike centralized exchanges, Bisq does not require identity verification.
glossary.def_boltzmann: A measure of the number of possible interpretations of a Bitcoin transaction's inputs and outputs. Higher entropy means more ambiguity and better privacy. Named after physicist Ludwig Boltzmann.
glossary.def_chain_analysis: The practice of tracing Bitcoin fund flows by applying heuristics to the public blockchain. Used by surveillance firms like Chainalysis and Elliptic to cluster addresses and deanonymize users.
glossary.def_change_output: The output in a Bitcoin transaction that returns unspent funds to the sender. Identifying change outputs links the sender to future transactions. Change detection is a core chain analysis technique.
glossary.def_cioh: The assumption that all inputs in a transaction belong to the same entity. The foundational clustering heuristic used by chain surveillance firms. CoinJoin is the primary way to break this assumption.
glossary.def_coin_control: A wallet feature that allows manual selection of specific UTXOs as inputs for a transaction, rather than relying on the wallet's automatic selection algorithm. Essential for privacy because it prevents accidentally co-spending UTXOs from different sources, which would link them via the Common Input Ownership Heuristic.
glossary.def_coinjoin: A technique where multiple users combine their transactions into one, breaking the common-input-ownership assumption. Implementations include Whirlpool (5 equal outputs) and WabiSabi (variable amounts, 20+ participants).
glossary.def_consolidation: A transaction that combines multiple UTXOs into fewer outputs (usually one), typically to reduce future transaction fees. Creates zero entropy and links all input addresses together via the Common Input Ownership Heuristic. For privacy, only consolidate UTXOs that are already linked, or run them through a CoinJoin first.
glossary.def_dust_attack: Sending tiny amounts of Bitcoin (dust) to target addresses. If the recipient spends the dust alongside other UTXOs, the attacker can link those addresses together, mapping the victim's wallet.
glossary.def_hd_wallet: A Hierarchical Deterministic wallet that generates a new address for each transaction from a single seed phrase. Avoids address reuse by default. Defined in BIP32/BIP44.
glossary.def_heuristic: A rule-of-thumb used to infer information about a Bitcoin transaction. am-i.exposed applies 30 heuristics to estimate what surveillance firms can deduce about any transaction or address.
glossary.def_hodlhodl: A non-custodial, peer-to-peer Bitcoin trading platform that uses 2-of-3 multisig escrow with the platform as a key-holding arbitrator. The platform never takes custody of funds. Trades create P2SH or P2WSH multisig outputs on-chain, with the multisig structure revealed when the escrow is spent.
glossary.def_joinmarket: A decentralized CoinJoin implementation using a maker-taker model. Makers offer liquidity and earn fees; takers pay for privacy. Creates transactions with varied input/output counts.
glossary.def_mempool: The set of unconfirmed Bitcoin transactions waiting to be included in a block. am-i.exposed fetches blockchain data from the mempool.space API (or a self-hosted instance).
glossary.def_multisig: A spending condition requiring M of N private keys to authorize a transaction (e.g., 2-of-3). Used for shared custody, escrow, and enhanced security. Legacy bare multisig is visible on-chain before spending. P2SH/P2WSH multisig reveals the structure only when spent. Taproot multisig using MuSig2 is indistinguishable from a single-signature spend on-chain.
glossary.def_op_return: A Bitcoin script opcode that embeds arbitrary data in the blockchain. Can leak metadata like timestamps, protocol identifiers, or messages that fingerprint the transaction.
glossary.def_payjoin: A transaction where both sender and recipient contribute inputs, breaking the Common Input Ownership Heuristic. Appears identical to a normal transaction on-chain - an observer cannot determine that two parties participated. Also called Pay-to-Endpoint (P2EP) or Stowaway (in Ashigaru). PayJoin V2 (BIP-77) operates asynchronously without requiring the recipient to run a server.
glossary.def_paynym: A user-friendly identity layer built on BIP47 reusable payment codes. Allows receiving Bitcoin without revealing addresses publicly. Used by Samourai and Sparrow wallets.
glossary.def_peel_chain: A pattern where a large UTXO is repeatedly spent, peeling off small payments and returning the remainder as change. Creates a traceable chain of decreasing outputs (e.g. 2.0 BTC -> pay 0.1, change 1.9 -> pay 0.2, change 1.7). Chain analysts use this to track funds across many transactions.
glossary.def_privacy_score: A 0-100 rating computed by am-i.exposed based on 30 heuristics. Starts at 70, adjusted by findings. Only CoinJoin, Taproot, and high entropy can raise it. Grades: A+ (90-100), B (75-89), C (50-74), D (25-49), F (0-24).
glossary.def_round_amount: A heuristic that identifies round-number outputs (e.g., 0.1 BTC, 1,000,000 sats) as likely payments, with the non-round output being change. Reveals spending patterns.
glossary.def_script_type: The address format used in a transaction (P2PKH, P2SH, P2WPKH, P2TR). Mixing script types in inputs or outputs can fingerprint change outputs since the change usually matches the sender's address type.
glossary.def_self_send: A transaction where one or more outputs return to an address that was also an input. This trivially identifies the change output, revealing the sender's remaining balance and the exact payment amount. HD wallets avoid this by generating a fresh change address for every transaction.
glossary.def_stonewall: A steganographic transaction format from Samourai Wallet (now Ashigaru) that mimics a CoinJoin. Has 2-4 inputs and exactly 4 outputs: 2 equal-valued outputs (one real payment, one decoy) and 2 change outputs. Solo Stonewall uses only the sender's UTXOs. STONEWALLx2 involves a collaborator contributing inputs, making it indistinguishable from a genuine 2-party CoinJoin.
glossary.def_sweep: A transaction that sends the entire balance of one or more addresses to a single output with no change. Common when migrating wallets or claiming funds. A single-input sweep (1-in-1-out) has zero entropy. A multi-input sweep additionally links all input addresses via the Common Input Ownership Heuristic. Provides zero unlinkability.
glossary.def_taproot: A Bitcoin upgrade (activated November 2021) that makes complex spending conditions look like simple ones on-chain. Improves privacy by making multisig, timelocks, and scripts indistinguishable from regular payments.
glossary.def_tor: An anonymity network that routes internet traffic through multiple relays. am-i.exposed auto-detects Tor Browser and routes API requests through the mempool.space .onion endpoint to hide which addresses are being queried.
glossary.def_utxo: Unspent Transaction Output - the fundamental unit of Bitcoin. Each UTXO is a discrete chunk of bitcoin that can be spent as an input in a future transaction. Managing UTXOs carefully is key to maintaining privacy.
glossary.def_wabisabi: A CoinJoin protocol used by Wasabi Wallet that allows variable-amount outputs using cryptographic credentials. Supports 20+ participants per round with flexible denomination selection.
glossary.def_wallet_fingerprint: Distinctive patterns left by wallet software - transaction version, locktime, sequence numbers, signature encoding - that reveal which wallet created a transaction.
glossary.def_whirlpool: A CoinJoin implementation by Samourai Wallet that creates transactions with exactly 5 equal outputs at fixed denominations (0.5, 0.05, 0.01, 0.001 BTC), achieving high entropy and strong privacy.
glossary.subtitle: Key terms and concepts for understanding Bitcoin on-chain privacy.
glossary.title: Bitcoin Privacy Glossary
