maintenance.intro: Good privacy is not a one-time achievement - it requires ongoing discipline. These practices help maintain the privacy gains detected in this analysis.
maintenance.network1: Connect your wallet through Tor to hide which addresses you query from the node operator.
maintenance.network2: Run your own Bitcoin node and mempool instance. This eliminates all third-party address queries.
maintenance.network3: Use a VPN or Tor when accessing block explorers in a web browser.
maintenance.postSpend1: Spend one UTXO per transaction whenever possible. Multiple inputs link addresses via Common Input Ownership.
maintenance.postSpend2: Never consolidate CoinJoin outputs. Each mixed output is an independent privacy unit - merging them undoes the mix.
maintenance.postSpend3: After CoinJoin, wait at least a few blocks before spending. Immediate post-mix spending creates timing correlation.
maintenance.spending1: Use Branch-and-Bound (BnB) coin selection for changeless transactions when possible. Bitcoin Core uses BnB by default.
maintenance.spending2: Match input and output script types (all P2WPKH or all P2TR). Mixed script types fingerprint the change output.
maintenance.spending3: For privacy-critical payments, prefer CPFP over RBF for fee bumping - RBF reveals which output is change.
maintenance.spending4: When making multiple payments, batch them into a single transaction. Multiple outputs increase ambiguity for change detection.
maintenance.spending5: Use Sparrow's 'Spending Privately' feature to construct Stonewall-like transactions that mimic CoinJoin structure using only your own UTXOs.
maintenance.title: Maintaining your privacy
maintenance.utxoHygiene1: Label every UTXO by source (exchange, P2P, CoinJoin, mining, payment). Never merge UTXOs from different sources.
maintenance.utxoHygiene2: Segregate KYC-sourced UTXOs from non-KYC. Treat them as separate wallets with separate spending strategies.
maintenance.utxoHygiene3: Freeze dust outputs (under 1,000 sats). Spending them costs more in fees than they are worth and can link your addresses.
maintenance.utxoHygiene4: If consolidation is necessary within a privacy category, time it during low-fee periods (under 5-10 sat/vB, often weekend nights UTC). Ensure resulting UTXOs are at least 1,000,000 sats to remain economically viable during fee spikes.
maintenance.wallet1: Stick with one wallet family to avoid mixing fingerprints. Switching wallets mid-UTXO-lifetime creates detectable patterns.
maintenance.wallet2: Ensure your wallet uses anti-fee-sniping (nLockTime = current block height) and standard nSequence values.
methodology.calculation_text: Every analysis starts from a base score of 70 - representing a typical Bitcoin transaction with no obviously good or bad characteristics. The base is above 50 because most transactions are not catastrophically bad; they carry the normal, baseline exposure of a transparent public blockchain.
methodology.calculation_text_suffix: - representing a typical Bitcoin transaction with no obviously good or bad characteristics. The base is above 50 because most transactions are not catastrophically bad; they carry the normal, baseline exposure of a transparent public blockchain.
methodology.cross_heuristic_intro: After all heuristics run, the engine applies cross-heuristic rules to avoid false penalties:
methodology.cross_rule_1_text: multiple input addresses in a CoinJoin belong to different participants, so the CIOH penalty is zeroed out
methodology.cross_rule_2_text: equal outputs in a CoinJoin are the denomination, not a privacy leak
methodology.cross_rule_3_text: change identification in CoinJoin transactions is unreliable and penalizing it would be misleading
methodology.cross_rule_4_text: mixed script types are expected when participants use different wallet software
methodology.cross_rule_5_text: wallet identification is less relevant when the CoinJoin already breaks transaction graph linkability
methodology.cross_rule_6_text: small outputs in CoinJoin transactions are typically coordinator fees, not dusting attacks
methodology.cross_rule_7_text: broadcast timing in CoinJoin is coordinated and does not reveal individual participant behavior
methodology.cross_rule_8_text: fee rate and RBF signals in CoinJoin reveal the coordinator, not the participant's wallet
methodology.cross_rule_9_text: CoinJoin structure provides privacy beyond simple output value matching
methodology.grade_aplus_description: Excellent privacy - CoinJoin participation, no reuse, high entropy
methodology.grade_aplus_detail: Uses advanced privacy techniques like CoinJoin that break deterministic transaction links. Minimal metadata exposure and strong anonymity sets make chain analysis unreliable.
methodology.grade_b_description: Good - minor issues, no critical exposure
methodology.grade_b_detail: No major privacy leaks detected. Minor issues like wallet fingerprinting or non-round change amounts may exist but do not enable confident tracing.
methodology.grade_c_description: Fair - notable concerns, moderate tracing risk
methodology.grade_c_detail: Some privacy concerns detected. An analyst could identify patterns like address reuse or change output detection, but the overall picture is still somewhat ambiguous.
methodology.grade_d_description: Poor - significant exposure, confident clustering likely
methodology.grade_d_detail: Significant privacy failures. Chain surveillance can likely cluster addresses and trace fund flows with moderate to high confidence.
methodology.grade_f_description: Critical - severe failures, trivial to trace
methodology.grade_f_detail: Severe privacy failures that make tracing trivial. Issues like heavy address reuse, round amounts, and identifiable wallet patterns create a clear picture for any observer.
methodology.heuristic_h10_description: Assesses the address format: Taproot (P2TR) hides script complexity, ideal for multisig and contracts. P2WPKH (native SegWit) has the largest anonymity set, excellent for single-sig. Legacy (P2PKH) and wrapped (P2SH) leak more information.
methodology.heuristic_h11_description: Identifies wallet software through nLockTime, nSequence, BIP69 ordering, and low-R signature grinding. Approximately 45% of transactions are identifiable by wallet software from structure alone.
methodology.heuristic_h12_description: Flags tiny UTXOs (under 1000 sats) as potential dusting attacks - an active surveillance technique where adversaries send small amounts that, when spent alongside other UTXOs, link addresses via CIOH.
methodology.heuristic_h13_description: Calculates how many outputs share the same value, making them indistinguishable. Higher anonymity sets (as in CoinJoin) mean more possible interpretations of fund flow. Outputs with unique values have an anonymity set of 1 and are trivially traceable.
methodology.heuristic_h14_description: Examines transaction timing patterns including broadcast recency (unconfirmed transactions risk IP correlation), locktime anomalies, and stale nLockTime values that can fingerprint wallet software.
methodology.heuristic_h15_description: Checks whether inputs and outputs use the same script type. Mixed script types (e.g., P2WPKH input with P2TR output) make change detection easier. Uniform script types improve ambiguity. Also flags bare multisig outputs.
methodology.heuristic_h16_description: Address-level analysis of spending behavior including transaction volume, UTXO turnover rate, and counterparty diversity. High-volume addresses or accounts that never spend may indicate specific usage patterns like exchanges or cold storage.
methodology.heuristic_h17_description: Parses wrapped multisig inputs (P2SH/P2WSH) to determine M-of-N configuration and detect escrow patterns. Identifies 2-of-2 escrow (P2P exchange or Lightning), 2-of-3 escrow (cold storage or P2P exchange), and HodlHodl fee address matches.
methodology.heuristic_h1_description: Flags outputs matching common round BTC or satoshi values. Round amounts typically indicate the payment (not change), letting an observer determine fund flow direction.
methodology.heuristic_h2_description: Identifies which output returns funds to the sender using address type matching, round amount analysis, and output ordering patterns. Also detects the critical case where outputs are sent back to an input address (self-send), revealing the sender's exact balance. Correct change identification enables multi-hop tracing.
methodology.heuristic_h3_description: If a transaction spends multiple inputs, all inputs are assumed to belong to the same entity. This is the foundational clustering heuristic - the single most powerful tool in chain surveillance. Applied transitively across transactions to build massive address clusters.
methodology.heuristic_h4_description: Detects Whirlpool (5 equal outputs at known denominations) and WabiSabi/Wasabi (large input/output sets with equal-value groups) CoinJoin transactions. CoinJoin is the only heuristic that increases the privacy score.
methodology.heuristic_h5_description: Measures how many valid input-to-output mappings exist. Higher entropy means more ambiguity for adversaries. Uses exact enumeration for small transactions and structural estimation for large ones.
methodology.heuristic_h6_description: Examines fee rate precision and RBF signaling. Round fee rates and specific RBF patterns help identify wallet software, reducing the anonymity set.
methodology.heuristic_h7_description: Identifies embedded data in OP_RETURN outputs - protocol markers (Omni, OpenTimestamps, Counterparty, Runes, Ordinals) and arbitrary text. Permanently visible metadata that distinguishes the transaction. Impact stacks with multiple OP_RETURN outputs.
methodology.heuristic_h8_description: Detects addresses that have received funds in more than one transaction. Address reuse is the single most damaging privacy behavior - it creates deterministic, irrefutable links between all transactions. Carries the harshest penalty, scaling with the number of reuses.
methodology.heuristic_h9_description: Evaluates the UTXO set for count, value distribution, dust detection, and consolidation risk. Large UTXO counts represent future privacy damage if consolidated carelessly.
methodology.heuristics_intro: The engine implements 30 heuristics that evaluate on-chain privacy - 24 at the transaction level and 6 at the address level. Each produces a score impact applied to a base score of 70.
methodology.limitation_1: Entropy calculation is simplified. Full Boltzmann analysis requires expensive enumeration that is impractical client-side for large transactions.
methodology.limitation_2: Only on-chain data is analyzed. Off-chain intelligence (IP correlations, exchange records, human intelligence) that surveillance firms use is not modeled.
methodology.limitation_3: Wallet fingerprinting covers major wallets but cannot identify all software. Novel or obscure wallets may not be detected.
methodology.limitation_4: Some privacy techniques (like PayJoin) are deliberately undetectable on-chain. A good privacy score does not guarantee privacy, and the absence of detected issues does not mean none exist.
methodology.subtitle: How your Bitcoin privacy is scored. Every heuristic documented, every penalty explained. The same techniques chain surveillance firms use - applied client-side to show you the results.
methodology.threat_1_text: group addresses controlled by the same entity using CIOH, change detection, and address reuse
methodology.threat_2_text: connect clusters to real people via KYC anchor points (exchange deposits, merchant payments)
methodology.threat_3_text: follow bitcoin through multiple hops using change detection and temporal analysis
methodology.threat_4_text: identify spending patterns, wallet software, timing, and financial activity
methodology.threat_model_intro: The analysis models the capabilities of chain surveillance firms (Chainalysis, Elliptic, CipherTrace) and KYC-linked exchanges. These adversaries:
methodology.threat_model_intro_prefix: The analysis models the capabilities of
methodology.title: Methodology
mistakes.coinjoinConsolidateDesc: Combining CoinJoin outputs in a single transaction re-links them via common input ownership heuristic (CIOH), undoing the entire mix.
mistakes.exchangeDirect: Send directly from exchange to final destination
mistakes.exchangeDirectDesc: Exchange withdrawal addresses are in chain analysis databases. Sending directly to your destination links the receiver to your exchange account.
mistakes.reuseAddress: Change wallet but reuse the receiver's address
mistakes.reuseAddressDesc: Switching wallets improves fingerprinting, but if you reuse the same receiving address, all prior transaction history is still linked.
mistakes.title: Common mistakes to avoid
mistakes.torOnly: Use Tor only without changing on-chain behavior
mistakes.torOnlyDesc: Tor protects your IP address, not your blockchain footprint. If your transactions still have round amounts, address reuse, and identifiable fingerprints, Tor alone does not help.
mistakes.wasabiThenSend: Mix with Wasabi then send immediately
mistakes.wasabiThenSendDesc: Wasabi's nVersion=1 fingerprint identifies the pre-CoinJoin transaction. Spending immediately after creates a timing correlation. Wait several blocks and use a different wallet for the spend.
page.aria_complete: Scan complete. Grade {{grade}}, score {{score}} out of 100.
page.description: Find out what the blockchain knows about you. Paste a Bitcoin address or transaction ID to get a privacy score with actionable findings.
page.tagline: The Bitcoin privacy scanner you were afraid to run.
pathways.bnb.con1: Only works when a UTXO combination matches the exact amount needed
pathways.bnb.con2: May require multiple UTXOs as inputs, triggering CIOH
pathways.bnb.desc: Use Branch-and-Bound coin selection to find UTXO combinations that exactly match the payment plus fee, eliminating the change output entirely.
pathways.bnb.pro1: No change output means change detection heuristic cannot fire
pathways.bnb.pro2: Bitcoin Core uses BnB by default
pathways.bnb.title: Exact Amount Spending (BnB)
pathways.cc.con1: Requires manual effort and discipline for every transaction
pathways.cc.con2: Not all wallets support coin control
pathways.cc.desc: Manually select which UTXOs to spend. Never merge KYC with non-KYC coins. Label everything by source and privacy context.
pathways.cc.pro1: Prevents accidental cross-context contamination via CIOH
pathways.cc.pro2: No additional tools or counterparties needed
pathways.cc.pro3: Works with any wallet that supports manual UTXO selection
pathways.cc.title: Coin Control & UTXO Hygiene
pathways.combined.intro: Think beyond single-tool solutions. The most effective privacy strategies combine multiple techniques across different layers.
pathways.combined.title: Combined pathways (strongest privacy)
pathways.combo.cjliq.steps: Mix first with CoinJoin, then peg into Liquid for confidential transactions.
pathways.combo.cjliq.strength: Combines CoinJoin anonymity set with Liquid's amount privacy. The peg-in links to a CoinJoin output, not your original identity.
pathways.combo.cjliq.title: CoinJoin -> Liquid
pathways.combo.cjln.steps: Mix UTXOs with CoinJoin, then open Lightning channels with mixed outputs. Payments through LN are off-chain.
pathways.combo.cjln.strength: On-chain mixing + off-chain spending. Channel opens are linked to CoinJoin outputs (which have high anonymity sets), not to your original funds.
pathways.combo.cjln.title: CoinJoin -> Lightning
pathways.combo.cjp2p.steps: Mix with CoinJoin, send through an intermediate address, then use for P2P purchase on Bisq or HodlHodl.
pathways.combo.cjp2p.strength: The intermediate hop prevents the P2P counterparty from seeing the CoinJoin directly. Multiple hops make tracing prohibitively difficult.
pathways.combo.cjp2p.title: CoinJoin -> Intermediate Hop -> P2P
pathways.combo.excjln.steps: Withdraw from exchange, CoinJoin the KYC-tainted UTXOs, then move to Lightning or Liquid for spending.
pathways.combo.excjln.strength: For KYC-sourced funds. CoinJoin breaks the link from exchange, then LN/Liquid adds another layer before final use.
pathways.combo.excjln.title: Exchange -> CoinJoin -> Lightning/Liquid
pathways.combo.lnliq.steps: Send via Lightning to Boltz Exchange, receive L-BTC on Liquid, then peg out to Bitcoin mainchain.
pathways.combo.lnliq.strength: Breaks the on-chain trail with two intermediate steps. The final BTC appears to come from the Liquid federation, not your original channel.
pathways.combo.lnliq.title: Lightning -> Liquid -> BTC
pathways.combo.xmr.steps: Swap BTC to XMR via atomic swap, hold in Monero, then swap back to BTC when needed.
pathways.combo.xmr.strength: Complete chain break. The receiving BTC has zero on-chain link to the original BTC. Strongest privacy option available.
pathways.combo.xmr.title: BTC -> Monero -> BTC
pathways.intro: On-chain privacy tools like CoinJoin are just one layer. The strongest privacy comes from combining multiple techniques across different networks.
pathways.jurisdictionNote: Privacy tool availability and legality vary by jurisdiction. Research your local regulations regarding CoinJoin, atomic swaps, and privacy coins before using these techniques. Some exchanges may flag or restrict accounts that interact with known privacy tools.
pathways.liquid.con1: Federated sidechain - requires trusting the Liquid federation members
pathways.liquid.con2: Peg-in and peg-out transactions are visible on the Bitcoin mainchain
pathways.liquid.con3: Smaller user base limits anonymity set
pathways.liquid.desc: Liquid is a Bitcoin sidechain with confidential transactions that hide amounts. Transaction amounts are encrypted and only visible to the sender and receiver.
pathways.liquid.pro1: Confidential transactions hide amounts from observers
pathways.liquid.pro2: Faster block times (1 minute) than mainchain
pathways.liquid.title: Liquid Network
pathways.ln.con1: Channel opens and closes are visible on-chain
pathways.ln.con2: Channel capacity reveals approximate balance range
pathways.ln.con3: Routing privacy depends on network path and node connectivity
pathways.ln.con4: Single-channel LSP dependency: if your wallet has only one channel, the LSP sees all payment amounts and destinations
pathways.ln.con5: Public channels advertise capacity and peers to the routing gossip. Use private (unannounced) channels when not routing for others.
pathways.ln.desc: Lightning payments happen off-chain, so they do not appear on the blockchain (except channel open/close transactions).
pathways.ln.pro1: Payments are off-chain and invisible to chain analysis
pathways.ln.title: Lightning Network
pathways.ln.warn1: Open channels with CoinJoin outputs for maximum privacy. Avoid opening from exchange withdrawal addresses.
pathways.ln.warn2: For maximum privacy, use Zeus connected to your own Lightning node. Phoenix and Breez route through single LSPs that can observe your payment activity.
pathways.pj2.con2: Adoption is still growing - limited counterparties
pathways.pj2.desc: Async, serverless PayJoin that breaks CIOH fundamentally. Both sender and receiver contribute inputs, making the transaction look like a normal payment on-chain.
pathways.pj2.pro1: Breaks Common Input Ownership Heuristic by design
pathways.pj2.pro2: Looks like a normal transaction - no CoinJoin fingerprint
pathways.pj2.pro3: Async protocol - receiver does not need to be online simultaneously
pathways.pj2.title: PayJoin v2 (BIP77)
pathways.sp.con2: Scanning for received payments requires checking every transaction
pathways.sp.desc: Publish one static address, receive unique on-chain Taproot outputs for each payment. No notification transaction needed, outputs are standard P2TR.
pathways.sp.pro1: Eliminates address reuse without out-of-band coordination
pathways.sp.pro2: Each payment creates a unique, unlinkable Taproot output
pathways.sp.title: Silent Payments (BIP352)
pathways.title: Privacy pathways beyond on-chain
pathways.xmr.con3: Slower than Lightning (on-chain settlement on both chains)
pathways.xmr.desc: Atomic swaps allow exchanging BTC for XMR and back, completely breaking all on-chain links between the two Bitcoin transactions.
pathways.xmr.pro2: Monero has built-in privacy (ring signatures, stealth addresses)
pathways.xmr.pro3: No trusted intermediary needed with atomic swaps
pathways.xmr.title: Monero Atomic Swaps
pathways.xmr.warn1: Regulatory risk: some jurisdictions restrict Monero. Research local regulations before using.
presend.adviceCritical: Do NOT send to this address. It poses severe privacy or legal risks.
presend.adviceHigh: Ask the recipient for a fresh, unused address before sending.
presend.adviceLow: This destination looks safe to send to.
presend.adviceMedium: Consider asking the recipient for a fresh address.
presend.disclaimerBrowser: Analysis ran entirely in your browser. This is a heuristic-based assessment - always verify independently.
presend.reusedAddressWarning: Sending to a reused address links your transaction to all other transactions involving this address. Chain analysis can trivially trace your payment.
presend.summaryCritical: This address has received funds {{reuseCount}} times. It is almost certainly a service or exchange deposit address - sending here will link your transaction to a known entity.
presend.summaryEscalated: This address appears unused. No significant privacy concerns detected for the recipient. However, heuristic analysis identified concerning patterns.
presend.summaryHigh: This address has received funds {{reuseCount}} times. All senders to this address are trivially linkable. Ask the recipient for a fresh address.
presend.summaryHighDataUnavailable: This address has {{txCount}} transaction(s) on-chain. Even without detailed receive data, this address has been used before. Do NOT send to this address - ask the recipient for a fresh, unused address.
presend.summaryHighReceivedOnce: This address has already received funds once. Do NOT send here - it will create address reuse, linking your transaction to the previous one on-chain. Ask the recipient for a fresh address.
presend.summaryHighReused: This address has received funds {{reuseCount}} times ({{txCount}} total transactions). Do NOT send here - your payment will be linkable to all previous transactions to this address. Ask the recipient for a fresh address.
presend.summaryLow: This address appears unused. No significant privacy concerns detected for the recipient.
psbt.incomplete: Some inputs are missing UTXO data. Fee calculation may be incomplete.
recent.title: Recent scans
recoveryFlow.intro: Follow these steps to improve your privacy score from Critical/F to Healthy/A:
recoveryFlow.step1Desc: Transfer your compromised UTXOs to Sparrow Wallet or Ashigaru. These wallets give you coin control and proper change address management.
recoveryFlow.step1Title: Move funds to a privacy-focused wallet
recoveryFlow.step2Desc: Run your funds through Whirlpool (Sparrow/Ashigaru) or JoinMarket to break the transaction graph. Each CoinJoin cycle adds anonymity set members.
recoveryFlow.step3Desc: After CoinJoin, let the outputs sit for at least 10-20 blocks. Spending immediately after mixing is a timing correlation signal that weakens your privacy.
recoveryFlow.step4Desc: Select individual UTXOs for each payment using coin control. Never combine multiple post-mix UTXOs in a single transaction - that undoes the CoinJoin.
recoveryFlow.step4Title: Spend with coin control - one UTXO per transaction
recoveryFlow.step5Desc: Always send to a fresh, never-used address from the receiver. If you control the receiving wallet, generate a new address for each receive.
recoveryFlow.title: How to recover from a bad score
remediation.avoidAddressReuseDetail: Use a new address for each payment you receive. Most modern wallets do this automatically.
remediation.avoidOpReturn: Avoid services that embed data in transactions
remediation.avoidOpReturnDetail: Some services leave permanent public data in your transactions (OP_RETURN). Use alternatives that don't leave metadata on the blockchain.
remediation.betterChangeHandling: Use a wallet with better change handling
remediation.betterChangeHandlingDetail: Use a wallet that sends change to the same address type as the payment (e.g., all bc1q). Ashigaru handles this automatically. Sparrow Wallet warns about type mismatches but does not correct them. For stronger protection, use Stonewall or PayJoin transactions which break the change detection heuristic entirely.
remediation.connectionPrivacyDetail: Your wallet's network connection reveals your IP and all addresses to the server. Basic: clearnet public server (for small amounts). Intermediate: connect via Tor to mask your IP. Advanced: run your own node with Tor for full sovereignty - the server never sees your queries.
remediation.continueCoinJoinDetail: Your CoinJoin gives strong privacy. Keep mixing with Whirlpool or Wasabi. Don't combine mixed and non-mixed funds.
remediation.doNotSpendDust: Do NOT spend this tiny output
remediation.doNotSpendDustDetail: Freeze it in your wallet's coin control. Spending it links your addresses together. If you must get rid of it, send it through CoinJoin first.
remediation.dust-attack.step1: Open your wallet's coin control and freeze this tiny output (mark it as 'do not spend').
remediation.dust-attack.step2: Never spend this with your other funds - it would link all your addresses together.
remediation.dust-attack.step3: If you must get rid of it, send it through CoinJoin or to a throwaway wallet.
remediation.fingerprintRandomization: Randomize your wallet fingerprint between transactions
remediation.fingerprintRandomizationDeta: Sparrow Wallet allows manually changing nVersion and nLockTime before signing (Headers > Version and Absolute Locktime fields). Alternating between nVersion=1/2 and different nLockTime values makes consecutive transactions look like they come from different wallets, breaking the fingerprint chain.
remediation.freshStart: Consider starting fresh with better privacy
remediation.freshStartDetail: Use a privacy-focused wallet (Sparrow, Ashigaru, Wasabi), create a new wallet, and send your funds through CoinJoin before moving them over. Use Tor for all Bitcoin activity.
remediation.h2-change-detected.step1: Avoid sending round amounts (like 0.01 BTC) - add some randomness so both outputs look similar.
remediation.h2-change-detected.step2: Use a wallet that keeps the same address format for all outputs, making change harder to spot.
remediation.h2-change-detected.step3: Spend your change separately from other funds to avoid further linking.
remediation.h2-change-detected.step4: Consider PayJoin V2 for your next payment - it works without needing a server and breaks change analysis.
remediation.h2-self-send.step1: Switch to a wallet that uses HD key generation - it automatically creates a new change address for every transaction.
remediation.h2-self-send.step2: Never manually set the change address to your sending address.
remediation.h2-self-send.step3: If your wallet does not support automatic change addresses, consider Sparrow Wallet or Bitcoin Core.
remediation.h2-self-send.step4: For funds already exposed by this pattern, consider using CoinJoin to break the linkability.
remediation.h3-cioh.step1: Use coin control in your wallet to pick which funds to spend - don't let it auto-select.
remediation.h3-cioh.step2: Avoid combining funds from different addresses unless they've been through CoinJoin first.
remediation.h3-cioh.step3: If you need to combine funds, run them through CoinJoin first to break the link between addresses.
remediation.h3-cioh.step4: Use a wallet that lets you label and control individual funds (Sparrow, Bitcoin Core).
remediation.h8-address-reuse.step1: Stop using this address now - never share it again.
remediation.h8-address-reuse.step2: Generate a new address in your wallet. Most wallets do this automatically for each receive.
remediation.h8-address-reuse.step3: Move remaining funds through CoinJoin to break the link to your history.
remediation.h8-address-reuse.step4: If CoinJoin isn't available, send funds to a new wallet through an intermediate address with a delay.
remediation.minimizeMultiInput: Avoid combining funds from different addresses
remediation.minimizeMultiInputDetail: Combining funds from different addresses in one transaction links them together. Pick specific funds when sending. If you must combine, use CoinJoin first.
remediation.segregatedSpending: Never mix KYC and non-KYC UTXOs
remediation.segregatedSpendingDetail: Use coin control to spend one UTXO at a time. Never combine inputs from different sources (exchange withdrawals, P2P purchases, CoinJoin outputs) in the same transaction. Label each UTXO with its source, risk level, and amount to make informed spending decisions.
remediation.smallChangeDisposalDetail: Small change outputs are toxic - they link future transactions back to this one. Options: send to a Monero atomic swap (UnstoppableSwap), open a Lightning channel with the change, or use a submarine swap service (LN <-> on-chain). Avoid letting small change sit in your wallet.
remediation.stopReusingAddressDetail: Get a new address for every payment you receive. Most wallets do this automatically. Move remaining funds to a new wallet through CoinJoin.
remediation.switchMultisig: Switch to Taproot for multi-key setups
remediation.switchMultisigDetail: Your current setup exposes all public keys on the blockchain. Taproot makes multi-key transactions look identical to single-key ones.
remediation.switchWalletSelfSend: Switch wallets - yours sends change back to the same address
remediation.switchWalletSelfSendDetail: Your wallet sends change back to the same address you spent from, destroying your privacy. Switch to Sparrow Wallet, Ashigaru, Bitcoin Core, or any HD wallet that generates fresh change addresses.
remediation.upgradeNativeSegwit: Upgrade to a Native SegWit (bc1q) wallet
remediation.upgradeNativeSegwitDetail: Native SegWit (P2WPKH, bc1q) addresses have the largest anonymity set of any address type, making transactions blend in with the majority of Bitcoin activity. They also have lower fees than legacy addresses. Sparrow Wallet, Ashigaru, and Bitcoin Core all default to Native SegWit.
remediation.useDecentralizedExchanges: Use decentralized exchanges for mixed funds
remediation.useDecentralizedExchangesDet: Many exchanges (Binance, Coinbase, Gemini, and others) flag or freeze accounts for CoinJoin deposits. Use decentralized alternatives (Bisq, RoboSats, Hodl Hodl) that don't track your history.
remediation.usePayJoin: Use PayJoin or CoinJoin to make transactions harder to trace
remediation.usePayJoinDetail: Simple transactions are easy to analyze. PayJoin adds inputs from the receiver, and CoinJoin mixes funds with other users - both make tracing much harder.
remediation.walletFingerprint: Use a wallet with a large anonymity set
remediation.walletFingerprintDetail: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - the goal is not invisibility but blending in with millions. Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, and Electrum have the largest user bases, making their fingerprints the least identifying. Wallets like Exodus or Trust Wallet have small, distinctive fingerprints that reveal poor privacy practices.
results.disclaimerBrowser: Analysis ran entirely in your browser.
results.disclaimerHeuristic: Scores are heuristic-based estimates, not definitive privacy assessments.
results.fGradeWarningAddr: This address has severe privacy issues. On-chain surveillance can likely identify the owner and trace fund flows. Immediate remediation steps are recommended below.
results.fGradeWarningTx: This transaction has severe privacy issues. On-chain surveillance can likely identify the owner and trace fund flows. Immediate remediation steps are recommended below.
results.scoringExplainerP1b: (baseline) and are adjusted by each heuristic finding. Negative findings (address reuse, change detection, round amounts) lower the score. Positive findings (CoinJoin, high entropy, anonymity sets) raise it.
results.scoringExplainerP3: The engine runs 30 heuristics based on published chain analysis research. Scores are clamped to 0-100. CoinJoin transactions receive adjusted scoring that accounts for their privacy-enhancing properties.
results.summaryFair: Some privacy concerns detected. Review the findings below.
results.summaryPoor: Significant privacy exposure detected. Remediation recommended.

setup.caddy_run: Then run caddy run in the same directory. In am-i.exposed settings, enter:

setup.manual_warning_2: Your URL must end with /api (e.g., http://localhost:3006/api)
setup.manual_warning_title: Two things must be true for manual setup
setup.remote_access_desc: If your node is on the same machine, use http://localhost:<port>/api directly. If it is on another machine on your network, use an SSH tunnel as described above.
setup.ssh_keep_open: Keep the terminal open while using the site. The tunnel stays active as long as the SSH session is running. You can add -N to the SSH command to skip opening a shell (e.g., ssh -N -L 3006:localhost:3006 ...).
setup.ssh_p1: This site is served over HTTPS. Browsers block HTTP requests from HTTPS pages (called mixed content) unless the target is localhost. An SSH tunnel forwards your node's mempool port to localhost on your machine, bypassing this restriction.
setup.ssh_replace: Replace user@your-node-ip with your node's SSH credentials. This maps port 3006 on your desktop to port 3006 on your node.
setup.ssh_settings: Then in the am-i.exposed settings (the gear icon), enter:
setup.start9_ca_desc: Your browser needs to trust the StartOS certificate authority. Download the CA from your StartOS dashboard and install it in your system/browser trust store. Without this, HTTPS requests to your .local address will fail.
setup.start9_config_desc: Use your mempool's LAN address in the settings:
setup.start9_config_replace: Replace <your-mempool-hostname> with the hostname shown in your StartOS dashboard for the mempool service.
setup.start9_cors_desc: SSH into your Start9 and edit the mempool nginx config to add the CORS headers shown above. The process is similar to Umbrel - find the running mempool container and edit its nginx config.
setup.start9_desc: Start9 serves mempool over HTTPS on a .local hostname with a self-signed certificate. There is no bare port to SSH tunnel to, so the approach is different from Umbrel.
setup.subtitle: Point am-i.exposed at your own mempool instance for maximum privacy. This guide covers Umbrel, Start9, Docker, and bare-metal setups.
setup.title: Connect Your Node
setup.tor_p1: If both am-i.exposed and your mempool instance are accessed via .onion addresses in Tor Browser, there is no mixed-content blocking (both are HTTP) and Tor Browser relaxes CORS restrictions for .onion-to-.onion requests.
setup.tor_p2: This requires a .onion mirror of am-i.exposed. If one is available, use Tor Browser to visit the .onion URL, then enter your mempool's .onion address in the settings.
setup.tor_p3: You still need CORS headers on your mempool nginx if the .onion addresses differ (which they will, since they are separate hidden services).
setup.ts_502_fix: Your mempool frontend (nginx) is reachable, but the backend is not responding. This usually means the mempool backend is still syncing the blockchain. Wait for it to finish and try again.
setup.ts_api_error: Health check passes but analysis returns no results
setup.ts_api_fix: Make sure your URL ends with /api. For example, http://localhost:3006/api - not http://localhost:3006. The app will warn you about this if it detects a missing suffix.
setup.ts_cors_error: "Connection failed" after setting up SSH tunnel
setup.ts_cors_fix: This is the #1 issue. Your SSH tunnel works at the network level, but your browser blocks the response because mempool's nginx does not include CORS headers. Add the CORS headers from Step 1 and reload nginx.
setup.ts_mixed_fix: You are entering an HTTP URL that is not localhost (e.g., http://umbrel.local:3006). Use an SSH tunnel to forward the port to localhost, then use http://localhost:3006/api.
setup.ts_restart_error: CORS changes lost after Umbrel restart
setup.ts_restart_fix: Umbrel recreates containers on updates. You need to re-apply CORS headers after each restart, or mount a persistent custom nginx config via Docker volume.
setup.ts_timeout_fix: Check that your SSH tunnel is still running (the terminal session must stay open). Verify the port number matches your mempool instance. Check firewall rules on your node.
setup.umbrel_app_desc: The easiest way. Install am-i.exposed directly on your Umbrel and it automatically connects to your local mempool instance. No CORS headers, no SSH tunnel, no configuration needed.
setup.umbrel_app_footer: The app detects your local mempool automatically. All API requests stay on your local network and Chainalysis lookups are routed through a built-in Tor proxy.
setup.umbrel_manual_config_apply: Click Apply. You should see a green checkmark if everything is configured correctly.

setup.umbrel_step4: Find am-i.exposed in the store and click Install
setup.verify_step1: Click the gear icon in the header and enter your URL (e.g., http://localhost:3006/api)
setup.verify_step2: Click Apply - you should see a green checkmark and "Connected. Using custom endpoint."
setup.verify_step3: Run an analysis on any transaction or address - results should load normally
setup.verify_step4: The gear icon shows an orange dot when a custom endpoint is active
setup.why_p1: When you use the public mempool.space API, their servers see your IP address and every address and transaction you query. This creates a log linking your network identity to your Bitcoin activity.
setup.why_p2: By pointing am-i.exposed at your own node, API requests never leave your local network.

taintFlow.title: Taint Flow
tips.0: Never reuse a Bitcoin address. HD wallets generate a new address for each receive automatically.
tips.1: CoinJoin breaks the common-input-ownership heuristic, making chain analysis significantly harder.
tips.2: Use Tor when broadcasting transactions. Your IP can be correlated with your on-chain activity.
tips.3: Round payment amounts (e.g., 0.01 BTC) reveal which output is the payment and which is change.
tips.4: Use native SegWit (bc1q) for everyday transactions - it has the largest anonymity set. Use Taproot (bc1p) for multisig and complex scripts where it hides spending conditions.
tips.5: Dust outputs (< 1000 sats) may be surveillance dust. Freeze them in your wallet's coin control.
tips.6: Wallet software can be identified through nLockTime, nSequence, and signature patterns.
tips.7: PayJoin has the receiver contribute an input, breaking the assumption that all inputs belong to the sender.

walletGuide.avoidCoinbaseWallet: Integrated with Coinbase exchange, queries go through Coinbase servers, no privacy features
walletGuide.avoidExchangeWallets: Custodial - the exchange controls your keys and sees all your transactions
walletGuide.avoidExodus: Clear fingerprint (nVersion=1, nLockTime=0), no coin control, no Tor, centralized servers
walletGuide.avoidTrustWallet: No coin control, no Tor support, sends all queries through centralized infrastructure
walletGuide.contradictionP1: Every wallet leaves a fingerprint - that is unavoidable. The goal is not to be invisible, but to be indistinguishable from millions of other users.
walletGuide.contradictionP2: A Bitcoin Core fingerprint is shared by millions of transactions. Knowing someone uses Bitcoin Core reveals almost nothing useful. An Exodus fingerprint, on the other hand, reveals poor privacy practices (no coin control, no Tor, centralized servers) and belongs to a much smaller set.
walletGuide.contradictionP3: Choose wallets where the fingerprint says "one of millions" rather than "one of a few with poor habits."

walletGuide.noCoinJoin1: Spend UTXOs individually - never combine inputs from different sources
walletGuide.noCoinJoin2: Use coin control to select specific UTXOs for each transaction
walletGuide.noCoinJoin3: Avoid consolidation transactions - they link your addresses together
walletGuide.noCoinJoin4: For mixing, consider Sparrow Wallet as a companion for CoinJoin before sending to hardware
